tip: DRY Principle: Don't Repeat Yourself. Extract common code into reusable functions or classes.
Scale horizontally by adding more servers rather than vertically upgrading a single server.
Write tests first (TDD). It helps design better APIs and catches bugs early.
Lazy loading: Load resources only when needed to improve app startup time.
Never store sensitive data like passwords or API keys in plain text or version control.
Use ViewModel to survive configuration changes and separate UI logic from business logic.
Use caching strategically: Cache at multiple layers (client, CDN, server) for best performance.
Keep functions small and focused. Each function should do one thing and do it well.
SOLID principles: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion.
Index your database columns that are frequently used in WHERE clauses for faster queries.
Use sealed classes for representing restricted class hierarchies and exhaustive when statements.
Implement rate limiting to protect your API from abuse and ensure fair resource usage.
Write meaningful commit messages: Start with a verb, keep it concise, explain the 'why' not the 'what'.
Avoid premature optimization. Profile first, then optimize the actual bottlenecks.
Use Jetpack Compose for modern UI development with declarative syntax and less boilerplate.
Version your APIs from day one. Use URL versioning (/v1/users) or header versioning.
Use meaningful variable names. Code is read more often than written—make it readable.
Use message queues for asynchronous processing and decoupling system components.
Follow the testing pyramid: More unit tests, fewer integration tests, even fewer UI tests.
Use Kotlin Coroutines for asynchronous programming—simpler and more readable than callbacks.
Use `@StateObject` to create and own an ObservableObject in SwiftUI. Use `@ObservedObject` only when the object is passed in from outside.
Always check `mounted` before using `BuildContext` after an `await` in Flutter, and check `view.window != nil` (or lifecycle state) before updating UI in async iOS callbacks.
Prefer `ListView.builder` over `ListView` with a `children` list for any dynamic or large list — it builds items lazily and is far more memory-efficient.
Mark struct methods that modify stored properties with `mutating`. This makes value-type semantics explicit and prevents accidental mutation on `let` instances.
Use `[weak self]` in closures that are stored or passed to external objects to break retain cycles. Only use `[unowned self]` when you can guarantee the object outlives the closure.
Use `ValueKey` or `ObjectKey` when dynamically adding, removing, or reordering widgets in a list. Without keys, Flutter matches widgets by position and can assign wrong state.
Swift's `async/await` with `URLSession.shared.data(from:)` makes network code linear and easy to read. It also integrates naturally with Swift's structured concurrency and `throws`.
Use `Dispatchers.Default` for CPU-intensive coroutines and `Dispatchers.IO` for blocking I/O. Never run heavy computation on `Dispatchers.Main` — it blocks the UI thread.
Prefer `StateFlow` over `LiveData` in new code. `StateFlow` works naturally in coroutines, has no lifecycle coupling in the ViewModel layer, and integrates cleanly with Compose.
Use `runTest` from `kotlinx-coroutines-test` to write fast, deterministic coroutine tests. Pair it with `TestDispatcher` to control virtual time and avoid real delays.
Apply Unidirectional Data Flow (UDF): state flows down from the ViewModel, events flow up from the UI. This makes state predictable and the app easier to test.
Use `context.watch<T>()` to rebuild when a Provider value changes, and `context.read<T>()` for one-time reads inside callbacks. Misusing `watch` in callbacks causes unnecessary rebuilds.
Core Data fetch requests are synchronous and block the calling thread. Always perform fetches on a background context (`newBackgroundContext()`) for anything non-trivial.
Unit test ViewModels without Android dependencies by injecting fake or mock repositories. Keep business logic in plain Kotlin/Swift classes — not tied to UI frameworks.
Run lint, unit tests, and build checks on every pull request in CI. Catching issues before merge is far cheaper than fixing them in production.
Use signed, versioned release builds in CI/CD pipelines. Store signing credentials in environment secrets — never commit keystore files or certificates to version control.
Add content descriptions to image-only UI elements so screen readers can describe them. In Compose use `contentDescription`, in UIKit use `accessibilityLabel`.
Ensure minimum touch target sizes of 48x48dp (Android) or 44x44pt (iOS) for interactive elements. Small targets cause frustration for users with motor impairments.
Use `Layout Inspector` in Android Studio and `View Hierarchy Debugger` in Xcode to diagnose unexpected UI layouts without guessing. Visual tools are faster than trial-and-error.
Add structured logging with log levels (DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR). Verbose logs in production consume battery and storage — use release build flags to strip debug logs.
Use `rememberSaveable` instead of `remember` in Compose when state must survive configuration changes like screen rotation or system-initiated process death.
Use `actor` types to protect mutable state shared across concurrent tasks. Actors serialize access automatically — no manual locking required.
`InheritedWidget` is Flutter's built-in mechanism for propagating data down the tree. `Provider` wraps it with a simpler API and reactive updates — prefer Provider for app state.
Separate your app into layers: UI, Domain (use cases), and Data (repositories). This makes each layer independently testable and reduces coupling.
Use `data class` for DTOs and model objects to get `equals`, `hashCode`, `copy`, and `toString` for free. Avoid using regular classes where structural equality is needed.
Write integration tests that cover the data layer — real database queries, real serialization. Unit tests with mocks can miss schema mismatches and codec bugs.
SwiftUI's `@EnvironmentObject` is ideal for app-wide shared state (e.g., auth session, theme). Inject it once at the root and read it anywhere without passing it through every view.
Automate version bumping and changelog generation with tools like `semantic-release` or `fastlane`. Manual versioning is error-prone and slows down release cycles.
Test your app with VoiceOver (iOS) and TalkBack (Android) enabled. Many accessibility issues are only discoverable by actually navigating the app without looking at the screen.
Use `const` constructors wherever possible in Flutter. Const widgets are never rebuilt unnecessarily, which is one of the simplest performance wins available.
Write the simplest correct version first, then improve it with evidence. Clear, working code is easier to test, profile, and safely optimize than clever code you do not fully understand.
Keep business rules independent of frameworks. UI, database, and network code should depend on the domain, not the other way around.
Make invalid states hard to represent. Clear types, explicit function parameters, and narrow data shapes prevent whole classes of bugs before runtime.
Choose data structures for the operations you need most. A simple change like using a set for repeated membership checks can turn slow code into predictable code.
Break problems into small, testable steps with clear inputs and outputs. Smaller pieces are easier to reason about, debug, and change safely.
Name concepts at the right level of abstraction. Precise names for data, functions, and modules reduce misread assumptions and make design problems visible earlier.
Validate external input at the edges of the system. Once data crosses a boundary, convert it into trusted internal types so the rest of the code can stay simple.
Estimate time and space complexity before you optimize. Big-O does not replace measurement, but it helps you rule out designs that will fall over as input size grows.
Before changing code, reproduce the problem and state what you expect to happen. Clear observations turn debugging from guesswork into a sequence of testable assumptions.
Prefer pure functions for core logic when possible. Code that depends only on its inputs is easier to test, reuse, and reason about.
Make invalid states hard to represent. When your data model encodes the real rules of the problem, entire classes of bugs disappear before runtime.
High cohesion means code that changes for the same reason lives together. Split modules when unrelated changes pull the same file in different directions.
Low coupling matters because every dependency is a change path. Fewer dependencies make code easier to test and safer to evolve.
Introduce an abstraction only when it hides real complexity or supports real variation. Extra layers without a purpose make code harder to read.
Refactor in small, behavior-preserving steps. Run tests after each step so design improvements do not turn into hidden rewrites.
A good test checks observable behavior, not private implementation details. Tests tied to internals break during refactors that keep behavior correct.
Reproduce the bug reliably before fixing it. A repeatable failure turns guessing into investigation.
Choose the simplest algorithm that meets the required scale. A theoretically faster algorithm is the wrong trade-off if it adds complexity without practical benefit.
Pick data structures by access pattern: lookup, insertion, ordering, and memory costs matter more than habit.
Time and space trade off against each other. Faster code often uses more memory, and memory-efficient code may do more work.
Shared mutable state is a primary source of concurrency bugs. Reduce sharing first; add locks or coordination only when necessary.
Immutable values are easier to reason about because they cannot change behind your back.
